metadata.source: Multiple web sources including World Bank, OECD, government statistics, regulatory bodies, and industry reports
metadata.methodology: Data compiled from official government publications, regulatory filings, World Bank reports, and academic research. All figures converted to GBP/USD as appropriate. Cost data adjusted to 2024/2025 prices where necessary. Timeline data reflects most recent publicly available project information as of April 2026.
metadata.disclaimer: Data represents latest available figures from cited sources. Some historical figures used where recent data unavailable. All figures rounded to reasonable precision given source uncertainty.

projects.note: Current estimate £81-100bn final cost. Highest construction cost per km globally.
projects.source: HS2 Ltd 6-monthly report to Parliament (March 2026), New Civil Engineer (March 2026)

projects.note: Ulm-Augsburg line €5.5bn for 85km. High tunnel percentage increases costs significantly.
Most cost-efficient high-speed rail in Europe. 2.5x cheaper than UK.
One-third lower than European costs. Standardized designs and strategic supply chain.

projects.source: Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT)

planningTimelines.category: Infrastructure Planning Approval Timeline (proposal to construction start)

uk.process: Development Consent Order (DCO) under Planning Act 2008

stages.description: Developer must consult extensively with local communities
Final decision and any post-decision challenges

uk.totalTimeline: 12-15 months formal process (18+ months total including pre-application)
uk.hsExample: HS2: 2010 proposal to 2017 Parliamentary approval = 7 years; 2017 approval to 2020 construction start = 3 years. Total 10 years proposal to construction.
uk.source: Planning Inspectorate, House of Commons Library, HS2 Ltd

france.process: Déclaration d'Utilité Publique (DUP) - Declaration of Public Utility

stages.description: Public consultation period with inquiry commissioner
State decision (prefectural decree or Council of State decree)
Period to execute land acquisition if needed

france.totalTimeline: 18-24 months to DUP approval (faster than UK)
france.source: French Government (Service Public), La Gazette des Communes

stages.description: Spatial impact assessment, route determination, stakeholder participation

germany.totalTimeline: 8-20 years for major projects (longest in peer group)
germany.note: Recent modernization efforts aim to simplify procedures and reduce timeline
germany.source: German Federal Ministry of Transport, Research Gate

denmark.timeline: 3-4 years from permit award to first power (realization phase)
denmark.note: Denmark uses streamlined processes for renewable energy. Fehmarn Belt Fixed Link (rail): construction began 2020, completion target 2029.
denmark.source: Danish Energy Agency, IEA, Railway Technology

planningTimelines.summary: UK DCO process (18 months formal, 10 years total with pre-application) vs France DUP (18-24 months) vs Germany (8-20 years). UK fast on paper but slow in practice with extensive pre-application requirements.

nuclear.category: Nuclear Power Plant Construction - Cost per GW and Timeline

projects.note: Massive cost overrun: originally estimated £18bn in 2015 prices
projects.source: EDF, World Nuclear News, Solar Power Portal

projects.constructionRate: Nearly all projects completed in 7 years or less since 2010
projects.note: Standardized designs, strategic supply chains, sustained government commitment. 55 operational reactors. 2024: 11 new reactors approved, avg $2.8bn each.
projects.source: World Bank, Anthropocene Institute, China Energy Portal

projects.constructionRate: 1/4 the cost of UK nuclear construction
projects.note: Sustained cost reductions through long-term government commitment and private contractor expertise development

nuclear.ukComparison: UK Hinkley Point C (£13.4bn/GW) is 7-8x more expensive than China (£1.8-1.9bn/GW) and South Korea (£1.7-1.8bn/GW), and similar to France Flamanville (£12.8bn/GW) which also massively overran.

uk.timeline: 7-10 years historically, improving to 3-4 years for recent projects
uk.note: 2024 saw first year without new completions since 2016; 2025 saw rebound with 1.3GW commissioned
uk.pipeline: 11.5 GW under construction (as of 2025), 2.6 GW planned for 2026
uk.source: Renewable UK, Offshore Wind, National Grid

denmark.note: Well-established permitting with EIA requirements. Infrastructure cost subsidy schemes available.

netherlands.timeline: 3-4 years realization phase (permitting reduced from 7-10 years)
netherlands.note: Significantly accelerated permitting in recent years

uk.perCapitaNote: Trend declining; 3Q 2025 only 37,500 starts (lowest since Q3 2014)

germany.lowestLevel: Lowest since 2015 completions, lowest permits since 2010

uk.reality: Only 19% of major applications decided within 13 weeks (2022-2024)
uk.compliance: One-third of local authorities failed to meet deadline on single major application
uk.source: Home Builders Federation, Planning on Empty report (Feb 2025)

transportProjects.category: Major Urban Rail Transit Projects - Timeline and Cost Overruns

projects.name: Crossrail / Elizabeth Line (London)
projects.source: House of Commons Library, NAO, Elizabeth Line operator

projects.name: Paris Grand Express
projects.firstSectionDelay: Pont de Sèvres section: 2020 planned → late 2026 actual
projects.note: 2x size of Crossrail, mainly tunneled. Delays attributed to testing issues.
projects.source: Major Projects Association, Railway Technology, Wall Street Journal

projects.name: Copenhagen Metro
projects.costFactor: Lower than London/Paris due to driverless trains, no barriers, value capture funding

projects.name: Munich S-Bahn (Second Core Route expansion)
projects.description: Doubling capacity with second parallel line and new tunnel under city
projects.costStatus: Specific costs not available in sources

transportProjects.costOverrunAnalysis: Crossrail +28%, Paris Grand Express +84% (cost estimate only). Copenhagen completed closer to budget using different delivery model (driverless, value capture). UK/France transport projects show significantly higher cost growth than Nordic comparators.

us.trend: Trump administration pushing significant cuts; 1,300+ Deloitte contracts terminated/slimmed (worth $372m)

eu.note: Only covers EU Commission; individual member state spending not aggregated

consultancy.summary: UK heavily dependent on external consultancies (£1.5bn annually with Big Four/MBB). Continental European governments employ stronger internal technical corps, reducing external consultant dependence.

civilService.category: Public Sector Employment as % of Workforce

uk.note: Civil service is only ~1.6% of total employed population, but represents growth trajectory (↑ 4.6% YoY June 2024)

france.context: Higher public sector presence than UK/Germany/Netherlands

civilService.note: Smaller public sectors in Germany/Netherlands correlate with higher reliance on external consultants. UK relatively balanced. France maintains larger public administration.

headlineComparisons.comparison: HS2 costs 13-14 times more per km than Spanish AVE network
UK nuclear construction costs 7x more than China's latest reactors

headlineComparisons.metric: Major rail transit cost overrun rate
headlineComparisons.comparison: Paris Grand Express +84% (4+ year delay). Copenhagen Metro closer to budget.
headlineComparisons.source: House of Commons Library, Major Projects Association

headlineComparisons.comparison: UK planning process 5x slower than France for major infrastructure

headlineComparisons.context: Continental Europe employs stronger internal technical capacity, reducing external dependence

headlineComparisons.metric: Urban metro construction cost per km
headlineComparisons.note: UK among most expensive globally alongside Netherlands

headlineComparisons.context: Both falling below replacement rate; Netherlands gap 410,000 homes
headlineComparisons.note: Shared planning/construction capacity crisis across UK/Germany

headlineComparisons.comparison: Netherlands cut timelines nearly 50%, UK plateaued

headlineComparisons.uk: 19% meet 13-week statutory deadline for major applications
headlineComparisons.context: One-third of local authorities cannot meet deadline on ANY major application

headlineComparisons.denmark: Driverless trains, no barriers, value-capture funding = £280m/km
headlineComparisons.lesson: Operational model and funding mechanism affects capital efficiency significantly
